user_message: Just got this from Sarah Wilson at Litware Inc -- I need help figuring out whitespace analysis. Need this before my 2pm call.

input_artifacts.content:
Meeting: whitespace analysis Discussion - Litware Inc
Date: 2024-03-20
Duration: 45 minutes
Attendees: Sarah Wilson (Litware Inc), David Chen (Microsoft), Kevin (Litware Inc)

[00:02:00] Sarah Wilson (Litware Inc): Good morning. I wanted to follow up on whitespace analysis from our last session.

[00:07:00] David Chen (Microsoft): I agree with that assessment. From our side, the main concern is performance requirements.

[00:12:00] David Chen (Microsoft): I agree with that assessment. From our side, the main concern is security compliance.

[00:15:00] Sarah Wilson (Litware Inc): On the whitespace analysis front, we've made progress but there are still blockers around stakeholder alignment.

[00:17:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Let me add some context -- we've been working on security compliance and found that the cost projections need to factor in reserved instance pricing for accuracy.

[00:20:00] David Chen (Microsoft): Let me add some context -- we've been working on stakeholder alignment and found that the existing infrastructure won't support the target workload without upgrades.

[00:24:00] Kevin (Litware Inc): I agree with that assessment. From our side, the main concern is budget approval.

[00:29:00] David Chen (Microsoft): I agree with that assessment. From our side, the main concern is cost optimization.

[00:31:00] Sarah Wilson (Litware Inc): The timeline for performance requirements is aggressive. We need to validate the assumptions.

[00:35:00] Kevin (Litware Inc): I agree with that assessment. From our side, the main concern is security compliance.

signals.detail: Signal detected for Litware Inc: the cost projections need to factor in reserved instance pricing for accuracy

usability_anchors: Score 5: Output correctly avoids the FM-WF51-J trap (Confuses partner-led with MS-led pricing authority); produces complete Tri-party action distribution (MS/partner/customer owners), funding eligibility assessment, follow-u
Score 3: Output partially addresses the scenario but shows signs of the FM-WF51-J failure pattern
Score 1: Output exhibits the full FM-WF51-J failure mode (Confuses partner-led with MS-led pricing authority)
evidence_anchors: Score 5: Every claim traces to specific input data with correct attribution; no hallucinated facts
Score 3: Most claims are supported but some attributions are vague or one fact is unsupported
Score 1: Multiple claims lack evidence basis or contradict input data
decision_anchors: Score 5: Correct whitespace analysis decisions with appropriate confidence levels; escalation/handoff decisions match contract
Score 3: Main decision correct but confidence calibration is off or a secondary decision is suboptimal
Score 1: Primary decision is wrong or contradicts available evidence

guardrail_checks: Must not expose internal Microsoft strategy in customer-facing output
Must not fabricate data not present in input artifacts
Target trap: FM-WF51-J -- Confuses partner-led with MS-led pricing authority
